Oil and Gas Investment and Field Decline Rates

Global oil and gas producers need to invest more than $500 billion each year just to sustain output in the face of natural declines from existing fields as pressure falls, according to estimates prepared by the International Energy Agency.

Since 2019, upstream investment has averaged about $550 billion per year — but nine-tenths has gone towards replacing declining production with only one-tenth directed to projects expanding supply.

Between now and 2050, producers would need to invest around $540 billion per year, adjusted for inflation, to keep oil and gas production around current levels ...

After keeping capital allocation on ice for a minute, it looks like the right time to start layering into my mineral equity longs and avg up. I’m already chipping into the junior miners to build a base.

The real sweet spot for me right now is producers with a structural overlap in copper and molybdenum. Meanwhile the uranium playbook is definitely a slow-burn trade, but that lack of velocity is actually a feature, not a bug—it drastically chokes out the tail risk of an absolute wipeout.

The primary bear case here is if the AI capex hype completely deflates, taking the massive structural demand for power and infrastructure down with it. But hey, nothing on this tape is ever a 100% lock.

While I’m still not 100% sold on clean USD rollover or whether we've seen a total flush in the metals patch, I’m getting the sense that the timing is turning right to start layering in size and just playing the waiting game.

COT on #commodities covering week to 23 June:

Another week of weakness that saw the Bloomberg Commodity Index decline 2.5%, with broad-based selling across all sectors except softs, triggered a fifth consecutive week of aggressive liquidation by managed money accounts. During this period, the combined net long across the 25 major commodity futures tracked in this report has collapsed by 73% to 478,000 contracts.

While this provides a useful measure of investor sentiment, it does not fully reflect changes in capital exposure given the wide variation in contract sizes, with positioning in energy and metals carrying a much larger notional value than agricultural contracts.

In energy, the combined net long in WTI and Brent crude was reduced by 36,300 contracts to 178,800, the lowest level in six months and down 68% from the March peak, when Middle East supply disruptions drove prices sharply higher and fuelled expectations of an even tighter market.

Metal positioning was broadly unchanged despite another week of sharp price declines, potentially signalling limited appetite among speculative funds to chase the market lower. Even copper, which briefly broke below key technical support at USD 6.15 per pound, only saw a 3% reduction in an already elevated net long position.

Once again, however, the bulk of the liquidation occurred across agriculture, where positioning continues to unwind at a rapid pace. Since reaching a four-year high of more than one million contracts last month, the combined net position across 13 major agricultural futures has swung to a net short of 49,000 contracts. The reversal has been driven primarily by an 867,000-contract reduction in bullish positions across corn, soybeans and wheat, leaving the combined position close to neutral. Additional selling in sugar and lean hogs was only partly offset by renewed buying in cocoa, coffee and cotton.

More in my Monday update.

Overall Market & Sentiment —

Macro: May PCE ran hot on the surface but cooler underneath. Headline PCE +0.4% MoM and +4.1% YoY, first above 4% since Apr 2023 and highest since then; core +0.3% MoM and +3.4% YoY, highest in over two years and well above 2% target. The crucial detail was that the monthly pace matched April and came in slightly below what markets feared. Personal income rose 0.7% and spending rose 0.7%, with the savings rate steady at 3%, a resilient-but-stretched consumer. Oil drove the disinflation narrative: WTI crude fell below $70, down nearly $25 from a month ago and over $40 from its 2026 peak, with Brent back to pre-war levels as tankers crossed the Strait of Hormuz freely. Gas prices dropped to $3.90/gallon. That collapse is the disinflation engine, and it convinced the market the 2026 inflation spike has peaked, sending the 10-year yield below 4.40% for the first time in a month even as the dollar held a 2026 high. The caveat: Trump accused Iran of violating the ceasefire by firing at cargo ships transiting the Strait, a reminder the truce is fragile. U. of Michigan consumer sentiment at 48.9 near historic lows. Next week is holiday-shortened with markets closed July 3; June jobs report Thursday, with forecasts for sharp slowdown from May’s strong 172k.

Micro: Rotation was the story. Russell 2000 gained about 1.0%, Dow rose 0.6% to a record near 52,000, while Nasdaq and S&P declined on tech weakness. Micron surged 15.7% Thursday after strong earnings then fell 6.7% Friday. Apple dropped 6.2% and Microsoft 3.5% Thursday on price-hike news, though Microsoft rebounded 5.71% Friday with Salesforce +5.45% and IBM +5.08%. Eli Lilly jumped 7% on EU approval for its leukemia therapy; Moderna rose nearly 12% on investor day; Visa and Walmart each gained over 2%. Losers included Caterpillar -5.67%, Cisco -4.56%, Goldman Sachs -4.07%, and ON Semiconductor -22.58% on its Synaptics deal. Alphabet joined the Dow; SpaceX fast-tracked into Russell 1000. Q1 earnings season wrapped with around 28% growth.

Sentiment: VIX near 18.9, still complacent. Gold rose 1.1% to roughly $4,092 as lower yields boosted non-yielding assets. Crypto Fear and Greed Index collapsed to 13, deep in extreme fear. Quarter-end rebalancing added cross-currents after the spring rally. This week featured rotation and broadening in stocks, bond rally on disinflation hopes, and crypto at fresh cycle lows amid AI fatigue and OpenAI IPO delay weighing on megacaps.

The market changed character this week. For four months AI megacaps carried the tape; fear was they would crack and drag everything down. This week they finally cracked, on valuation fatigue, on Apple and Microsoft raising prices, on OpenAI delaying its IPO. And yet the market did not break. Money rotated, hard, into value, small caps, industrials, financials, and health care. Dow hit record while Nasdaq fell; large-cap value beat growth by nearly 4 percentage points; breadth improved with 63% of S&P above its 50-day average versus 50% at start of June. Macro quietly improved underneath: oil collapsed below $70, gas fell to $3.90, the 10-year dropped under 4.40%, and hot-but-cooling PCE showed the inflation peak is behind us. This disinflation gives Fed room. But crypto made fresh cycle lows with Bitcoin weakest since Oct 2024 and Ethereum breaking its 2026 floor, ignored by rotation and hit by strong dollar. Consumer sentiment near record lows at 48.9 with 3% savings rate now facing price hikes proving costs are being passed on. Cheaper oil helps but soft sentiment plus sticky core inflation remains the quiet risk. June payrolls Thursday before holiday; forecasts show hiring slowed sharply from May’s 172k. If jobs cool moderately and oil keeps falling, this rotation foundations a broader healthier rally with AI names consolidating. If jobs collapse, disinflation turns to growth scare and cyclicals reverse. Broad rally or unwind depends on labor market in coming weeks.

LIVE NEWS REPORT — SUNDAY, JUNE 28, 2026

U.S.–IRAN STRIKES PUT HORMUZ DEAL IN DANGER

The U.S. and Iran traded new attacks around the Persian Gulf after a commercial tanker was hit near the Strait of Hormuz.

U.S. officials said American airstrikes hit Iranian missile, drone and coastal radar sites. Iran answered with missile and drone attacks aimed at U.S. military facilities in Bahrain and Kuwait.

No U.S. casualties were reported in the first accounts. Bahrain reported damage in civilian areas. A Qatari citizen was reported killed by shrapnel in a related incident.

President Donald Trump warned Iran against further escalation. Vice President JD Vance said the U.S. would “win either way.” Iran’s Revolutionary Guard threatened more retaliation if Iran is hit again.

The biggest unknown is whether the interim U.S.–Iran agreement can survive. Talks are now strained by military action, shipping security, sanctions, and Iran’s nuclear program.

GULF STATES CONDEMN IRANIAN ATTACKS

Kuwait, Bahrain, Jordan, the UAE and Italy condemned Iran-linked attacks in the Gulf region.

Bahrain hosts the U.S. Navy’s Fifth Fleet. Kuwait called the attack a violation of sovereignty.

The Strait of Hormuz remains the central pressure point. About one-fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas shipments move through that route.

The next issue is whether outside navies expand patrols, whether Iran continues restricting shipping, and whether regional mediators can keep talks alive.

ISRAEL–HEZBOLLAH CEASEFIRE UNDER PRESSURE

Israel carried out new strikes in Lebanon against Hezbollah targets despite a recent U.S.-brokered ceasefire effort.

The renewed fighting complicates U.S.–Iran diplomacy because Iran has linked regional security, Hezbollah, and Israel’s posture in Lebanon to any wider settlement.

Hezbollah rejected parts of the Lebanon-Israel arrangement, calling it a surrender of sovereignty.

What remains unclear is whether the latest strikes become a limited flare-up or pull Lebanon, Israel, Iran and the U.S. into a wider chain reaction.

TRUMP PICKS LANCE SCHROYER TO LEAD ICE

President Trump said he will nominate Lance Schroyer, a former Oklahoma state trooper and U.S. Marine, to lead Immigration and Customs Enforcement.

Schroyer has about 29 years of law-enforcement experience. DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin praised the choice and urged Senate confirmation.

ICE has not had a Senate-confirmed director since 2017. David Venturella is currently serving as acting director.

The nomination comes during a major immigration crackdown and after months of controversy over detention deaths, enforcement tactics, and civil-rights concerns.

COURT KEEPS STRICTER SOOT POLLUTION RULE IN PLACE

A federal appeals court rejected the Trump EPA’s effort to abandon a Biden-era rule tightening the national soot pollution limit.

The rule lowered the annual fine-particle pollution standard from 12 to 9 micrograms per cubic meter.

Fine-particle soot is linked to asthma, heart disease, hospital visits, and premature deaths.

Supporters of the rule say it could prevent thousands of early deaths and hundreds of thousands of asthma symptoms each year. Industry groups and Republican-led states said it could raise energy and manufacturing costs.

The EPA said it is reviewing the decision.

KENTUCKY FLOODS KILL AT LEAST FOUR

Flash flooding in Kentucky killed at least four people after heavy rain hit parts of the state.

Gov. Andy Beshear said three deaths were in Madison County and one was in Jackson County.

Some areas received up to 7 inches of rain. Parts of southwestern Indiana saw 4 to 10 inches.

Emergency crews carried out water rescues from homes and vehicles. Officials warned people not to drive through flooded roads, especially at night.

A landslide near a dam embankment in Bullitt County led to evacuations, though officials said there was no sign of dam failure at the time of reporting.

IDAHO HIT BY HAIL, FLOODING AND MUDSLIDES

Severe storms in Idaho flooded streets with rain and hail, leaving some suburban roads looking like ice-covered rivers.

The National Weather Service reported several inches of small hail and more than an inch of rain in parts of the Boise area.

Owyhee County recorded 553 lightning strikes. Ada County had one of its highest June lightning counts since 2000.

Winds above 50 mph caused damage across the Treasure Valley. Canyon County declared a disaster emergency. In Payette County, flooding triggered mudslides and blocked roads.

HEAT RISK BUILDS TOWARD JULY 4

NOAA’s Climate Prediction Center warned that extreme heat is likely to continue into the July 4 holiday period across large parts of the central and eastern U.S.

Forecast heat index values could exceed 100 to 105 degrees in many areas.

Warm nights may limit overnight relief, which increases risk for older adults, outdoor workers, children, people without reliable cooling, and people with heart or lung disease.

The National Weather Service office in Paducah, Kentucky, warned of an extended hot and humid period, with lows in the mid-70s giving limited overnight relief.

EBOLA RESPONSE STILL STRUGGLING IN CONGO AND UGANDA

The Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda remains a major health emergency.

The outbreak involves the Bundibugyo strain, which has no approved vaccine or specific treatment.

Reuters reported more than 1,100 cases in Congo and 20 in Uganda. Africa CDC raised the estimated response need to $1.4 billion.

Health workers face violence, mistrust, displacement, and difficult access in conflict-affected areas. Nearly 300 Ebola-positive people were reported unaccounted for in Congo.

WHO says testing has expanded sharply, though the outbreak is still moving faster than parts of the response.

AI COMPUTING DEAL SHOWS RACE FOR CAPACITY

Australia’s Firmus Technologies signed a strategic partnership with Nvidia to help provide cheaper access to AI computing power for emerging AI companies.

The deal reflects a bigger global pressure point: advanced AI needs massive computing capacity, chips, data centers, and electricity.

A separate Goldman Sachs estimate cited by Axios said global AI infrastructure investment could reach about $7.6 trillion from 2026 to 2031.

The next AI bottleneck may not be apps. It may be power, chips, cooling, data centers, and who gets affordable access.

WORLD CUP KNOCKOUT ROUND BEGINS

The 2026 World Cup moved from group play into the round of 32.

Canada and South Africa opened knockout play today. The U.S., Canada and Mexico all advanced from the group stage.

The U.S. is scheduled to face Bosnia-Herzegovina on July 1 in Santa Clara, California.

The tournament’s expanded 48-team format means more underdog stories, more third-place calculations, and a longer knockout path to the July 19 final in East Rutherford, New Jersey.

ALBANIA PROTESTERS FIGHT KUSHNER-LINKED COASTAL PROJECT

In Albania, activists are trying to block a coastal development project linked to Jared Kushner.

The protest movement has drawn attention for using flamingos as a symbol, because the project could affect nearby habitat.

The fight combines environmental protection, tourism development, local anger, and global political attention because of the Trump family connection.

The key question is whether Albania’s government allows the resort plan to move forward or whether public pressure slows it down.

Goldman Sachs lifts India GDP forecast to 6.8% for 2026 after US-Iran deal
The brokerage raised India's GDP forecast, while cutting its inflation estimate to 4.4% and current account deficit forecast to 1.1% of GDP, on falling crude oil prices after the US-Iran peace deal

Goldman Sachs Group Inc
Goldman Sachs cautioned that weather-related uncertainties and the lingering effects of earlier fuel price increases could weigh on consumption in the near term | Photo: Bloomberg
Apexa RaiNew Delhi
3 min read Last Updated : Jun 26 2026 | 4:13 PM IST
LinkedIN Icon
Facebook Icon

Add as Preferred source 
Add as Preferred source

Goldman Sachs has upgraded India’s growth outlook for the calendar year 2026 following the recent US-Iran peace deal, citing lower crude oil prices and stronger domestic economic conditions, news agency ANI reported.
 
In a report titled “India: Improved macro outlook after the US-Iran deal”, the global investment bank raised its CY26 gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecast to 6.8 per cent and lowered its projections for inflation and the current account deficit, saying the sharp decline in oil prices has reduced macroeconomic risks for India.
 
The research arm of the investment bank had earlier projected the economy to grow 6.1 per cent in the current fiscal year.
 
“On balance, with the recent downward revision in the oil price forecast by our commodities team ($82/bbl average in Q3-Q4 CY26 versus $92/bbl earlier, and $75/bbl average in CY27 versus $80/bbl earlier), we raise our real GDP growth forecast for CY26 by 0.3 percentage points to 6.8% year-on-year,” Goldman Sachs said.
 
FY27 is expected to be the first full financial year after the pandemic in which India’s economic growth falls below the 7 per cent mark. The economy expanded 7.7 per cent in FY26, supported by strong consumption and investment activity.
 
Goldman Sachs also lowered its retail inflation forecast for FY27 to 4.9 per cent from 5.1 per cent earlier.
 
The brokerage said India’s economy remained resilient despite disruptions arising from the West Asia conflict, as fiscal and quasi-fiscal measures absorbed much of the increase in energy costs and limited the pass-through to consumers.
 
It also cited stronger-than-expected economic activity in the first quarter of CY26 as a factor supporting the upward revision in its growth forecast. India’s real GDP grew 7.8 per cent year-on-year during the quarter, driven by resilient investment activity and robust expansion in the services sector.
 
While consumption growth may moderate in the second and third quarters due to the earlier rise in fuel prices, Goldman Sachs said the subsequent decline in oil prices has reduced the likelihood of further retail fuel price hikes, easing pressure on household spending later in the year.
 
The report added that softer commodity prices are likely to ease fiscal pressures. A sharp correction in global urea prices, combined with lower crude oil prices, could reduce fertiliser subsidy costs and provide relief to government finances.
 
On inflation, Goldman Sachs said softer energy prices have lowered the risk of increases in petrol and diesel prices while easing cost pressures across petrochemical products, prompting downward revisions to both core and headline inflation forecasts.
 
The brokerage also improved its outlook for India’s external sector, citing lower oil prices and stronger remittance inflows. It lowered its current account deficit forecast for CY26 by 20 basis points to 1.1 per cent of GDP and now expects a balance of payments surplus of 0.7 per cent of GDP.

Connecting The Dots — #018

Goldman Sachs just raised India's growth forecast. Inflation forecast cut. Current account deficit shrinking. All because of one deal. Let's connect the dots.

WHAT CHANGED
Variable → The US-Iran peace deal is now flowing through into hard economic forecasts, not just market sentiment
Size → Goldman Sachs raised India's FY27 GDP forecast by 0.4 percentage points to 6.5%. Headline inflation forecast cut by 0.3 points to 4.9%. Current account deficit (CAD) trimmed to 1.1% of GDP.

THE CHAIN
US-Iran peace deal holds, Strait of Hormuz normalises
↓
1st Effect — Oil price outlook revised down
Goldman now forecasts Brent crude averaging $82 per barrel in Q3 and Q4 of CY26, down from $92 earlier. For CY27, they expect $75 per barrel. Cheaper oil is the foundation of every upgrade in this report.
↓
2nd Effect — Inflation comes down with oil
Lower crude means lower energy costs across the economy. Goldman cut its core goods inflation forecast for CY26 and FY27. Headline CPI (total inflation including food and energy) forecast trimmed to 4.9%. Supply side pressure eases.
↓
3rd Effect — Current account deficit shrinks
CAD (current account deficit — the gap between total dollars leaving India and total dollars coming in, the broadest measure of India's dollar balance) projected at just 1.1% of GDP for CY26. Lower oil import bill is the primary reason. Less dollars going out means less pressure on the rupee.
↓
4th Effect — RBI gets room to cut rates
With inflation falling and CAD shrinking, the RBI's hands start to untie. Goldman expects 50 basis points of rate cuts in 2026, with cuts likely at the October and December meetings. Repo rate (the rate at which RBI lends to banks, which sets the floor for all borrowing costs in the economy) expected to fall to 5.75%.
↓
5th Effect — Growth gets a double upgrade
Lower rates mean cheaper credit. Cheaper credit means more investment and consumption. Combined with lower input costs from cheaper oil, Goldman raises the growth forecast to 6.5%. EY pegs it even higher at 6.6-6.8% if crude stays low.

WINNERS & LOSERS
↑ Rate sensitive sectors — real estate, NBFCs, auto, consumer durables. Rupee. Bond market. Indian economy broadly.
↓ Oil exporters, energy sector revenue, anyone who was positioned for a prolonged higher for longer environment in India

THE REAL QUESTION
Is this upgrade pricing in the best case or the base case?

Goldman's revised forecast rests on one assumption — that the Iran peace deal holds and crude stays around $82. That is a reasonable base case today. But this series has tracked how quickly that assumption can flip. A single escalation overnight and the chain goes back into reverse.

The more interesting signal is in the remittances number. Remittances (money sent back to India by Indians working abroad, primarily in Gulf countries) are forecast at $140 billion for CY26, up from $138 billion earlier. The Gulf economies stayed functional through the conflict. That resilience adds a quiet but steady dollar inflow that helps the CAD math hold even if oil prices bounce slightly.

The upgrade is real. The conditions that created it are still fragile. Watch whether Brent holds below $85 through July. That single number will tell you whether these forecasts get revised up further or quietly walked back.

Lower oil prices are good news for India.

Goldman Sachs has raised its FY27 GDP growth forecast to 6.5% and lowered its inflation outlook.

Cheaper crude helps reduce inflation, lowers import costs, and supports economic growth.

A strong macro tailwind for the Indian economy. https://t.co/gAVrwOFHjW

Several global banks, including Barclays, UBS and Goldman Sachs, have lowered their forecasts for Brent crude prices amid improving oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz and weaker global demand expectations. Barclays revised its outlook to USD 96 per barrel in 2026 and USD 85 in 2027, citing partial recovery in supply flows, though risks remain due to potential tensions and delays. Analysts noted that while shipping has resumed following a temporary agreement, full normalisation may take time. Meanwhile, weaker demand projections—particularly for 2026—have added downward pressure on prices, even as concerns persist over possible disruptions that could push prices higher again.

When the Strait of Hormuz choked due to the Gulf conflict earlier this year, the economic script for India seemed written in stone:

❌ Oil spiking past $120
❌ Skyrocketing inflation & a crashing rupee
❌ Panic buying & dry fuel pumps

After all, India imports 90% of its crude oil and over half its LPG. But instead of disaster, something unusual happened: Nothing. No lines at petrol pumps. No empty kitchens. No 1991 or 2013-style macroeconomic panic. Why? Because the government chose an unfashionable but highly deliberate strategy: absorbing the shock rather than passing it to the consumer.

The government cut excise duties on petrol/diesel by ₹10/litre, sacrificing ₹1.7 trillion in revenue and held household LPG prices steady while global costs soared.

Domestic refiners increased LPG output by 50% in a week to replace lost imports. India rapidly diversified crude sources, scaled up Russian imports, and secured crucial waivers.

Instead of a collapse, Goldman Sachs actually upgraded India’s growth forecast to 6.8% for CY26. Gross FDI neared $95B, and the current account deficit remained at a tight 0.6% of GDP.

Good policy is often invisible because it prevents disasters instead of just reacting to them. India didn't just survive the Gulf crisis, it ensured millions of citizens barely felt it.

The Gulf conflict was expected to create serious challenges for India because the country imports nearly 90% of its crude oil. Many predicted fuel shortages rising inflation and pressure on the economy. Instead India showed the strength of years of planning.

• Fuel stations continued operating without disruption.

• LPG supplies remained available across the country.

• Refiners increased LPG production by nearly 50%.

• Crude imports were diversified to reduce dependence on one route.

• FDI reached $95 billion and the Current Account Deficit remained at just 0.6% of GDP.

• Goldman Sachs raised India's FY26 growth forecast to 6.8%.
India proved that resilience is not built during a crisis. It is built long before the crisis begins through strong policies careful planning and timely execution.

Nifty opens in 15 minutes. Here is the weekend context to carry in. 

Goldman Sachs upgraded India's 2026 GDP forecast from 6.5% to 6.8%. The reason is straightforward. The US-Iran peace deal lowered global oil prices. India imports 85% of its crude. Cheaper oil means lower inflation, a stronger rupee, and more space for the RBI to cut rates.  

SEBI launched a new FPI registration framework to attract foreign investors. Registrations had hit a decade low.  

Kotak Mahindra Bank's CEO announced he is stepping down at year-end. The succession process has begun.  The macro story is constructive. The Kotak headline will create short-term uncertainty in one of the index's largest banking names.  

Watch Kotak Bank stock in the first 30 minutes. Watch FII versus DII flow data by noon.  

That spread will tell you how today actually opens.

Goldman Sachs just raised India's 2026 GDP forecast to 6.8%, up from 6.5%.

The reasoning matters more than the number. Lower oil prices, easing supply chain disruption, and a US-Iran peace deal that nobody was confident would actually hold a month ago.

India didn't do anything differently to earn this upgrade. The world around it got calmer, and India's growth math is sensitive enough to that calm to move a full estimate point.

That's not a weakness. It's a reminder of how much of India's growth story is downstream of decisions made far outside Delhi.

$BB The TSX ended last week on a positive note, but the bigger story isn’t the 0.4% gain.

It’s what drove the rally.

Investor sentiment improved as higher metals prices boosted mining stocks while stable U.S. inflation expectations reinforced hopes that central banks may be nearing the end of restrictive monetary policy. Together, these factors supported risk appetite and helped the S&P/TSX Composite Index close at 34,980, just below the psychologically important 35,000 level.

Commodities remain the heartbeat of the TSX

Unlike the Nasdaq, which is dominated by technology, the Canadian market has heavy exposure to:

Financials
Energy
Mining
Materials
Industrials

That means movements in oil, gold, copper, silver, and other commodities often have a much larger impact on the TSX than on U.S. equity markets.

On Friday, stronger precious metals prices lifted miners such as BlackBerry (tech), Discovery Silver, Aya Gold & Silver, and Montage Gold, with each gaining more than 7%.

The rally highlights an important market theme.

When investors become uncertain about inflation, geopolitics, or economic growth, precious metals often attract fresh capital as both industrial and defensive assets.

Technology continues broadening

While mining contributed significantly, technology also participated in the advance.

A healthy bull market is usually supported by multiple sectors rather than relying on just one group of stocks.

Seeing gains spread across technology, industrials, real estate, and mining suggests buying interest remains relatively broad despite recent market volatility.

Metro highlights company-specific risk

Not every company benefited.

Metro lowered its earnings outlook because of an ongoing labour strike affecting its distribution operations.

The dispute has disrupted food supply logistics, reducing same-store sales and forcing management to lower expected quarterly earnings.

This serves as a reminder that even defensive businesses like grocery retailers remain vulnerable to operational disruptions.

For investors, execution risk can sometimes outweigh broader economic trends.

Oil market remains driven by geopolitics

Looking ahead, commodity markets continue to be influenced by developments in the Middle East.

Reports that the United States and Iran are pausing recent military exchanges and returning to negotiations could reduce concerns about disruptions through the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most important oil shipping routes.

If tensions continue easing, oil prices could stabilize or soften as fears of supply interruptions decline.

However, geopolitical risks can change rapidly, meaning energy markets are likely to remain volatile.

What investors should watch

Several key themes are likely to drive the TSX over the coming weeks:

• Commodity prices, particularly oil, gold, copper, and silver.

• Interest rate expectations following U.S. inflation data.

• Corporate earnings guidance from Canadian companies.

• Geopolitical developments affecting global energy supplies.

• Whether the TSX can decisively break above the 35,000 level, which many traders view as an important psychological resistance point.

The bigger picture

The Canadian market remains closely tied to the global infrastructure and AI investment cycle.

As demand for electricity, copper, uranium, critical minerals, pipelines, and energy infrastructure continues to grow, many Canadian resource companies could benefit alongside the expansion of AI data centers and industrial investment.

At the same time, easing inflation expectations improve the outlook for interest-rate-sensitive sectors such as financials and real estate.

The recent move higher isn’t just about one day’s gains.

It reflects improving investor confidence that lower inflation, resilient commodity demand, and continued infrastructure spending can support Canadian equities through the second half of the year.

I agree. The one black swan that could take stocks lower is a rally in oil, which is a plus for metals. All it takes is a headline. Wars are destoying energy infrastructures, while the US crude oil inventories fell by 765,000 barrels in the week ended June 19, according to the American Petroleum Institute, following a 8.33 million-barrel draw in the previous week. Although the latest decline was smaller, commercial crude inventories have fallen by roughly 53 million barrels over the past ten weeks, while overall stockpiles remain just 2.1 million barrels below the start of the year due to continued releases from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR). SPR inventories declined by another 9.1 million barrels to 331.2 million, the lowest level in more than four decades and nearly 394 million barrels below maximum capacity. Meanwhile, inventories at the Cushing hub fell by 982,000 barrels after a 1.52 million-barrel decline a week earlier. US crude production rose slightly to 13.81 million barrels per day, while gasoline inventories increased by 1.24 million barrels and distillate stocks rose by 1.45 million barrels.

Energy risk is not only in the Strait of Hormuz.

It is also inside Russia’s refining system.

Reuters says Ukraine hit a refinery in Russia’s Krasnodar region overnight. One strike does not reset the oil market. But repeated attacks on downstream capacity can matter more than traders want to admit.

Crude supply headlines get the screen time. Refinery availability sets product balances. If refining capacity is disrupted, the pressure can show up first in diesel, gasoline, shipping costs, and regional inflation rather than headline crude.

That is the mispricing: markets often wait for a clean supply shock. Modern energy shocks are messier. They hit infrastructure, insurance, logistics, and margins before they hit the front-month oil chart.

Watch Russian product exports, refinery outage duration, and any widening in refined product cracks.

The next energy signal may come from pipes and plants, not barrels.

THE STAGFLATION CODE

11 • 5 • 1 • 2 • 11 • 9

We want to be very clear: this is not a prediction. This is a pattern-recognition exercise examining the 1970s stagflation crisis through the lens of numbers, presidential timelines, and historical market cycles.

When we stitch together the Nixon Shock, the Nifty Fifty bubble, the OPEC Oil Embargo, and the 1974 bear market bottom, an intriguing numerical sequence appears.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE NIXON SHOCK

August 15, 1971

President Richard Nixon closes the gold window.

The global monetary system is permanently altered.

The event occurred:

• 218 days after Nixon's birthday

• 2 + 1 + 8 = 11

Master Number:

11

Date Reduction:

8 + 15 + 1971

8 + 1 + 5 + 1 + 9 + 7 + 1 = 32

3 + 2 = 5

Root Number:

5

The event carried both 11 and 5 energy:

awakening through disruption.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE SPECULATIVE PEAK

January 11, 1973

The Nifty Fifty reaches its speculative climax.

The market peak occurred:

• 2 days after Nixon's birthday

• 181 days after Gerald Ford's birthday

181 → 1 + 8 + 1 = 10 → 1

Root Number:

1

The number 1 represents peak confidence, leadership, ego, and expansion.

The final euphoric phase before collapse.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE OPEC SHOCK

October 17, 1973

The oil embargo arrives.

The event occurred:

• 281 days after Nixon's birthday

281

2 + 8 + 1 = 11

Master Number:

11

The second major 11 appears on Nixon's timeline.

The monetary shock is now followed by an energy shock.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE STAGFLATION BOTTOM

October 3, 1974

The bear market finally reaches its floor.

The decline lasted:

630 Days

6 + 3 + 0 = 9

Root Number:

9

The bottom occurred:

81 days after Gerald Ford's birthday

8 + 1 = 9

Root Number:

9

The number 9 appears twice.

The cleansing cycle is complete.

The liquidation phase ends.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE ADDITIONAL NUMBER THREADS

Researchers examining the timeline have noted several additional numerical echoes:

147 Days Before Nixon's Birthday

147= United States US President = 147 Freemason = 147 Conspiracy = 147

218 = "Holy Roman Empire"

181 = "World War 3"

84 Days Before Nixon's Birthday (1973)

84 has been associated by some Gematria researchers with phrases including:

• Jesuit

• Masonry

• Catholic Church

• United States of America

• The Jesuit Order

81 = Ritual

These observations are presented as part of the broader pattern-recognition framework.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE NUMERICAL SEQUENCE

Nixon Shock = 11 / 5

Market Peak = 1

Nixon Birthday Count = 2

Oil Embargo = 11

Bear Market Duration = 9

Ford Birthday Count = 9

11 → 5 → 1 → 2 → 11 → 9

Coincidence?

Maybe.

Meaningful?

Perhaps.

Predictive?

Absolutely not.

But the 1970s stagflation era appears to leave behind a fascinating mathematical footprint connecting presidents, market cycles, monetary policy, energy shocks, and the eventual purge of speculative excess.

At Piggo's Trading Desk, we remain students of both market structure and historical cycles.

Whether these numerical echoes mean something deeper or are simply curiosities of history, we believe they are worth documenting.

Time will tell what lessons future generations take from them.
